title: Γεωργὸς καὶ ὄφις ὑπὸ κρύους πεπηγώς
titleEn: The Farmer and the Frozen Snake
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 82 (= Perry 176). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Γεωργός τις χειμῶνος ὥρᾳ ὄφιν εὑρὼν ὑπὸ κρύους πεπηγότα, τοῦτον ἐλεήσας καὶ λαβὼν ὑπὸ κόλπον ἔθετο.
sentences.tr: Geōrgos tis cheimōnos hōrāi ophin heurōn hypo kryous pepēgota, touton eleēsas kai labōn hypo kolpon etheto.
sentences.l: A farmer certain, in-winter's season a snake having-found by frost stiffened, him having-pitied and having-taken-up, under his-bosom placed.
sentences.i: One winter's day a farmer found a snake stiff with frost, and taking pity on it, picked it up and put it in his bosom.
sentences.n: χειμῶνος ὥρᾳ — the same winter opening as the Cicada and the Ants. πεπηγότα is the perfect participle of πήγνυμι 'fix, freeze solid' (the title's πεπηγώς): the snake is a frozen object, not yet an actor.

sentences.t: Θερμανθεὶς δὲ ἐκεῖνος καὶ ἀναλαβὼν τὴν ἰδίαν φύσιν ἔπληξε τὸν εὐεργέτην καὶ ἀνεῖλε·
sentences.tr: Thermantheis de ekeinos kai analabōn tēn idian physin eplēxe ton euergetēn kai aneile:
sentences.l: But warmed-through, that-one, and having-taken-back the own nature, struck the benefactor and killed-him:
sentences.i: But once warmed through, the snake recovered its own nature, struck its benefactor, and killed him.
sentences.n: ἀναλαβὼν τὴν ἰδίαν φύσιν, 'taking back its own nature' — the fable's whole theory of character in four words. εὐεργέτης, 'benefactor': the word the Lion and the Mouse built its happy ending on, here made a victim.

sentences.t: θνῄσκων δὲ ἔλεγε· « Δίκαια πάσχω, τὸν πονηρὸν οἰκτείρας. »
sentences.tr: thnēiskōn de elege: « Dikaia paschō, ton ponēron oikteiras. »
sentences.l: and dying he-said: « Just-things I-suffer, the wicked-one having-pitied. »
sentences.i: And as he died he said: « I suffer justly — I took pity on a villain. »
sentences.n: δίκαια πάσχω: the neuter plural as internal object, 'I suffer just things' = I get what I deserve. The aorist participle οἰκτείρας names the deed the punishment answers.

sentences.t: Ὁ λόγος δηλοῖ ὅτι ἀμετάθετοί εἰσιν αἱ πονηρίαι, κἂν τὰ μέγιστα φιλανθρωπεύωνται.
sentences.tr: Ho logos dēloi hoti ametathetoi eisin hai ponēriai, kan ta megista philanthrōpeuōntai.
sentences.l: The story shows that unchangeable are the wickednesses, even-if with-the greatest-things they-be-treated-kindly.
sentences.i: The story shows that wickedness does not change, however great the kindness shown it.
sentences.n: κἄν = καὶ ἐάν by crasis: 'even if.' φιλανθρωπεύωνται — 'be treated with philanthrōpia': the fable weighs a whole ethical vocabulary, φύσις against φιλανθρωπία, and lets nature win.
